generations.text:
**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the ghosts of those I’ve lost. The sea roars in fury, and I fear the night may claim me before dawn. But if I should not survive, I pray this letter finds you—though I know it may never reach you, and I would not blame you if it does not.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not dared to write. I feared the words would not be enough, and worse, that they would not be welcome. You were young when you left, and I was… I was a fool. I let pride and silence build a wall between us, and I have spent these years in regret. I have not been the father you deserved, nor the man you once believed in.

I do not know if you still remember the nights we spent by the fire, when you were a child and I would read you stories of the sea. You used to say the lighthouse was like a great, watchful eye, always watching over us. I used to think it was the light that kept the storm at bay. But now, I see it was you—your light, your kindness, your unyielding hope—that kept me from falling into the darkness.

I have not been a good man, Eleanor. I have made choices that have hurt you, and I have not sought to make amends. But I want you to know this

**Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a wounded beast, rattling the shutters of this old tower, and the sea roars as though it too mourns the distance between us. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, to the past, to the daughter I once held close in my arms.

I have not forgotten you, Eleanor. I have not forgotten the laughter that once echoed through these stone walls, nor the way your eyes would light up when you saw the lighthouse beam pierce the dark. You were my light, my hope, my greatest joy. And yet, I let you go. I let the silence grow between us, and I have not spoken a word to you since.

I know you think me cruel, that I abandoned you. But I ask you now, with the weight of this storm upon my soul, to understand. I was a man of duty, bound not only to this tower but to the sea, to the lives that depended upon its watchful eye. I could not leave my post, even when my heart ached for you. I tried, Eleanor. I tried to write, to visit, to find a way to mend the rift between us. But the sea, as ever, was a cruel mistress, and it took more from

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this by the dim glow of the oil lamp, its flickering light casting long shadows upon the walls of this old lighthouse. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, that ever-faithful companion, roars in defiance of the heavens. Tonight, the storm is coming with a vengeance, and I fear the sea may claim me before the dawn.

Five years have passed since I last saw you, and I have not sent a single letter. I know not if you have heard of me, or if you have forgotten me entirely. Perhaps you have. Perhaps you have not. But I write now, not as a man who seeks forgiveness, but as a father who must speak before the world is gone.

I have always believed that the sea is a cruel and fickle mistress, but I have never thought it could be so unkind as to take away the one thing that has kept me from drowning in my own sorrow—my hope that you might one day return.

You were young when you left, Eleanor. Young and full of fire, with dreams that burned brighter than the lantern I keep burning night after night. You said you needed to see the world, to find your own path. I did not stop you. I could not. And perhaps I should have. But I thought, if I let you go, you might find the life you were meant to live.

And yet, I have watched the

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a banshee at my window. The sea is restless tonight, and the waves have begun to claw at the cliffs with a fury I have not seen in years. I do not know if I will live to see dawn, but if I do, I must say these words, for they have been weighing on my heart like a stone.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left, and five years since I have dared to speak your name without the weight of sorrow pressing down on me. I have tried to forget, to pretend that the day you walked out of our home was just another storm that passed. But the storm has not passed. It has only grown.

You were a child then, no older than twelve, and yet you spoke to me as though I were a stranger. I remember the look in your eyes—cold, hard, and full of something I could not name. I thought I had done wrong, that I had failed you in some way. But I have come to understand now that it was not me you were angry with, but the world, and the life we were meant to have.

You were always different, Eleanor. Not in the way others are, but in a way that made me proud. You had a fire in your soul, a light that burned too bright for this world. I thought I could protect you, but I was wrong

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. Five long years have passed since the last time we spoke, and though I have often thought of you, the silence between us has grown like the sea—vast, unyielding, and ever deep.

The wind howls now at the edges of the cliff, and the sea, as restless as ever, roars against the rocks below. I have stood here for many a night, watching the waves crash and the stars blink out one by one, and yet tonight feels different. There is a storm gathering, one that does not merely threaten the lighthouse, but the very soul of this place. I fear for my life, and with it, I fear for the life I have led—alone, as you once said I was.

You were always the light, Eliza. Even when you were not here. I remember the nights you used to sit by the fire, your voice soft as the lullaby you once sang to me. You were the one who taught me how to read the stars, how to see the beauty in the storm, how to find peace in the chaos. I miss that. I miss you.

I know you have your own life now, your own path to walk. I do not blame you for choosing it. You were always a child of the world, while I have remained here, bound to the sea and the stone. But

**My Dearest Eliza,**

It is with a heart heavy as the storm clouds that I sit to write to you. Five years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and yet, the weight of that silence still presses upon me like the tide upon the rocks. I have not spoken to you in so long, and I fear that time has not softened the wounds we both carry.

Tonight, the sea howls with a fury I have not known in many a year. The wind screams like a banshee, and the waves, with their relentless wrath, seem to rage against the very walls of this lighthouse. I have lit the lamp, as I have done every night since the day you left, and I have stood guard through the darkness, not only for the safety of those who sail these waters, but for the hope that perhaps, one day, you will return to me.

You were young when you walked away, and I was too proud to say the words that would have eased your pain. I told myself that you were better off without me, that I was not the father you deserved. But I have come to see that I was wrong. I was afraid to show you my weakness, afraid that you would see in me the man I have become — a man who has spent his life in solitude, watching the sea, and forgetting how to speak the language of love.

I do not know if you still remember the nights we spent by the fire,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It has been five long years since I last laid eyes upon you, and I fear the time has only grown heavier with each passing day. The sea, ever restless, has whispered to me in the night, and I have heard in its murmurs the echoes of your name. Tonight, the storm is coming—fierce, unrelenting, and I do not know if I shall see dawn.

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lighthouse. The lantern has been lit, as it must be, though I cannot help but wonder if it is for the sea or for you. You were always the light in my life, Eleanor, and I have long since forgotten how to shine without you.

I have not spoken to you in years, and I know you have not spoken to me. I have no excuse for the silence, no justification for the distance that has grown between us. I was a fool to think I could keep the sea at bay with my own stubbornness, just as I was a fool to think I could keep my heart from breaking when you left. You were only twelve, and I was too proud, too afraid, to tell you the truth. I was not a father in the way you needed me to be. I was a man who believed that duty was more important than love, and in doing so, I lost you.

I have spent these years

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since you left, and though the days have worn on, the night still finds me staring into the abyss of the sea, watching the waves crash against the rocks as if they were your footsteps, echoing through the hollows of my soul.

You were once the light in my life, my little star, and I have not seen it since that fateful evening you turned your back on me. I have tried, oh how I have tried, to reach out, to call to you, but your silence has grown like a wall, tall and unyielding, and I have not dared to climb it.

Tonight, the storm is coming. The sky is dark as a funeral shroud, and the wind howls like a beast in pain. I have seen the signs, the way the sea roars and the air grows thick with the scent of impending ruin. I fear that this night may be the last I see of the world. And yet, I find myself writing, as if the ink might carry my words to you, as if the sea might carry them to your heart.

I have no regrets, Eleanor. I have always been a man of duty, of responsibility, and I believed that my role as a lighthouse keeper was greater than any personal desire. I thought that by keeping the light burning, I was ensuring your safety, your future

**April 12th, 1897**  
**Lighthouse, Point Grey, Scotland**

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time I saw you, and I fear that this may be the final letter I shall ever send. Tonight, a storm is coming—one like none we have seen in these parts. The sea roars like a beast awakened, and the wind howls as if it seeks to tear the very sky from its moorings. I have seen many storms in my time, but this... this feels different. I do not know if I shall see dawn.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza. I know you have blamed me for your mother's passing, and I can only imagine the pain you must have felt. I was not the man I wished to be, and I did not act with the love or wisdom you deserved. I have spent those years in guilt, in silence, and in the solitude of this lighthouse, watching the sea with a heart that aches for what was lost.

But now, as the storm approaches, I find myself thinking of you more than ever. I have spent countless nights gazing at the horizon, wondering if you ever looked in that direction, if you ever thought of me. I have not written, not spoken, not even sent a single letter. And I know

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*April 5th, 1892*  
*St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isles of Scilly*

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The sea roars, and the sky is a curtain of black, heavy with the weight of a storm that has not yet touched the shore. I do not know if I will see dawn. But if I do, I will not be the same man who has stood at this window for the last five years, watching the waves with the bitter taste of silence in my mouth.

You have not written to me, not once. Five years. I have not spoken your name aloud in the stillness of the night, fearing it might awaken the ghosts that have haunted me since the day you left. I have told myself that time would mend what words could not, that the sea would carry your memory away, and that I would one day find peace in the quiet of this place. But the sea is not kind, and neither is the silence.

I have always believed in the light. It has guided ships through the darkest nights, through the most treacherous of waters. I have stood here, alone, for so many years, and I have come to believe that the light is not just a tool of navigation—it is a promise. A

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast outside these stone walls, and the sea roiling as though it too knows the weight of what I must say. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have spent each of them haunted by the silence between us. Tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to reach out—not to beg for forgiveness, but to make amends, if it is still possible.

You were but a child when you left, and I, a man who had long since forgotten how to love. I had thought I could protect you, guide you, but I failed. I was too rigid, too proud, too blind to see the girl you had become. You were not the child I had raised, but a woman with her own heart, her own dreams, and her own path. And I, in my stubbornness, did not let you go.

I have not spoken your name in five years, but I have not forgotten it. I have not forgotten the look in your eyes when you left—full of sorrow, but also of something else: hope. I have wondered, night after night, whether that hope was for me or for yourself. I have spent these years trying to understand, but I fear I have only grown more lost.

Tonight, as the storm threatens to swallow the lighthouse whole, I am reminded of the first time I saw you.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write these words with a heavy heart, my quill trembling in my hand, for I know not whether it will reach you or be lost to the sea as so many have been before it. The wind howls like a banshee, and the waves, they roar as if the very heavens are in turmoil. Tonight, the storm is upon us with a fury I have not seen in my years of keeping this lighthouse. I fear for my life, and with it, I fear for the peace of my soul.

Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not written, nor have you answered. I know you have your own life, your own cares, and perhaps, your own regrets. I have not sought you out, for I have been a fool, and I have been afraid. Afraid of what you might say, of what you might think of me, of what I might say to you.

You were once my greatest pride, my brightest star in a world of endless night. I watched you grow, and I watched you leave, and I did not know how to call you back. I thought, perhaps, that time would mend what had been broken, that silence would heal what had been wounded. But time has passed, and the silence remains.

I have not forgotten the day you left. I remember it as if it were yesterday—the rain, the cold, the way your eyes looked upon me

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time I set eyes upon you, and though the world has turned, I fear the tide of time has not washed away the wounds we both bear.

Tonight, the sky has darkened with a fury that even the most seasoned mariners would call unnatural. The sea, which has been my constant companion for over thirty years, now roars with a voice that sounds almost like a warning. The wind howls through the stone walls of this lighthouse as if it were a ghost from the past, and I find myself wondering if this storm is but a mirror to the tempest that has raged between us.

I have no illusions, Eleanor. I know you would not have come back, not even if the sea had parted for you. You left with your own grief and your own pride, and I, in my stubbornness, allowed it to fester. I have not spoken your name in all this time, not even in my prayers, and yet I find myself thinking of you more than I care to admit.

You were but a child when you left, and I, a man too proud to ask for what I needed most. I told myself I was protecting you, that I was keeping you safe from the world’s cruel winds, but in truth, I was only keeping myself safe from the truth of my own heart.

**[Sealed with wax, the letter bears the faint scent of salt and old parchment]**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the wind howling like a banshee and the sea roiling as if it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. The lantern is lit, as it has been for thirty years, and the light will guide the ships through the darkness. But tonight, I fear it may not be enough to save me.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five years of silence, of longing, of watching the horizon for a ship that will never come. I have not heard your voice, nor seen your face, and yet I feel your absence in every gust of wind, in every wave that crashes against these rocks. I have tried to be the man you once knew—steady, strong, and unshaken. But I am not the man I was.

You were always the light in my life, my little star, shining bright even when the world was dark. I remember the nights you’d come to the lighthouse, when you were but a child, and you’d sit with me by the fire, asking questions about the stars and the sea. You had a way of making the world seem smaller, of turning the vastness of the ocean into something we could both understand. I used to tell you that the lighthouse was not just a beacon for the ships, but a beacon for the soul—

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret, for I have not penned a single word to you in five long years. Time has been a cruel companion, and the sea, ever restless, has taken more from me than I ever thought possible. Tonight, the storm approaches with a fury that even the most seasoned mariners would fear, and I find myself questioning whether I shall see the dawn.

You were but a child when we last spoke, and I, a man consumed by duty, by the relentless demands of this lighthouse and the sea that watches over it. I told myself that I was protecting you, that I was doing what was right, but in truth, I was running. From the memories, from the guilt, from the truth that I had let you go. I was a fool, Eleanor, and I have paid the price in silence.

I have not forgotten you. Not a single day has passed without your face appearing in my dreams, or your voice echoing in the wind that howls through the tower. I have kept your portrait in the lantern room, tucked away where the salt air cannot reach it, and I have often stared at it as I tend to the light, wondering if I would ever find the courage to reach out.

The storm is upon us now, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I have lit the beacon, as I have done for thirty years, and I have never felt

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavier than the stone walls of this lighthouse. Five years have passed since you left, and though the sea has not forgotten me, I fear I have forgotten how to speak your name without the weight of regret pressing down like the tide.

The wind howls now, a sound that echoes the storm that rages beyond these cliffs. I've lit the lamp, as I always do, though its glow seems dimmer tonight, as though the very light of the world is waning. I’ve spent my life watching the sea, but never have I felt it so close, so insistent, as if it knows I am not as strong as I once believed.

I’ve not heard from you in five years, not a single letter, not a single word. I tried to write, once, but the ink blurred and the paper crumpled in my hands. I could not bring myself to send it. I told myself it was for your sake, that I would not burden you with my failures. But now, as the storm gathers, I wonder if I have been the one who has been burdened all along.

You were so young when you left, Eleanor. Just a girl with fire in her eyes and a heart full of dreams. I remember the day you stood at the door, your coat still damp from the rain, your eyes searching mine for something I could not give. I

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with the ink trembling in my hand, for my hands have grown unsteady of late, and the wind outside howls like a beast in torment. The sea is restless, and the sky is bruised with clouds that promise nothing but ruin. I have not seen you in five years, and I fear that when the storm breaks, I may not live to see another dawn.

I have kept this lighthouse these many years, standing firm against the tempests that have swept across the horizon. It is a solitary duty, one that has taught me patience, and silence, and the weight of time. I have watched the stars fade, and the moon wane, and the tides rise and fall with a rhythm older than memory. Yet, in all of that, I have never seen the light of your face.

You left without a word, as you always have. I do not know if it was anger, or sorrow, or some cruel misunderstanding that drove you away. I have tried, in my quiet way, to forgive. But I have not forgotten. You were my child, and I have always loved you, though I have not known how to show it.

The wind has grown louder, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that stirs something deep within me. I have seen many storms, but this one feels different. It is not merely the sea that threatens me, but the silence that has settled between us

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It is with a heart heavy with both sorrow and hope that I take up my quill to write to you. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and I fear the silence has grown like the sea—deep, unyielding, and ever creeping. I have not forgotten you, Eleanor, and though I have done my best to keep our memories at bay, they return to me with the wind, the waves, and the distant cries of gulls.

The night is dark, and the sky has turned the color of a storm-tossed sea. The wind howls as though it carries the voices of the dead, and I can feel the very air trembling with the promise of a terrible tempest. I have spent the evening in the lighthouse, securing the lantern, checking the oil, and ensuring that the light will shine through the night. It is my duty, and I have always taken it to heart. But tonight, I feel the weight of it more than ever. I am not so young as I once was, and the sea, in its cruel wisdom, has not spared me.

I have not written to you because I have feared what might come of it. I have feared that my words would not reach you, or worse, that they would not be heard. You have been distant, Eleanor, and I have been a fool to believe that silence could ever be mistaken for peace. I have not been the same since

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of the damned, and the sea raging as though it seeks to swallow the very foundations of this old tower. The lantern’s light flickers, and I fear it may not hold through the night. I have spent many years in this lonely place, but never have I felt so alone as I do now, with the storm gathering on the horizon and the weight of my silence pressing upon me like the tide.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since you left me. I have not heard from you, not a single word, not even a letter. I suppose you have your own life to live, your own path to follow. But I have not forgotten you. I have not forgotten the day you stood at the door, your face pale, your eyes filled with sorrow, and you said, *“I can’t stay here, Papa. I can’t.”* And I did not argue. I did not try to hold you back. I simply said, *“Then go, my child. But remember, I will always be here, waiting for you.”*

And I have been waiting, Eleanor. Waiting for you to return, to speak, to forgive me. But you have not come. And I have not written. I have not dared to write, for fear that the words would be too heavy, too full of regret, too much of

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink of a man who has long since lost the certainty of his own hands, and with the heart of a father who has spent a lifetime yearning for the chance to speak to you once more. The wind howls like a beast in the night, and the sea, that ever-faithful companion, roars in protest as if to remind me that time has not been kind to either of us.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of watching the waves crash against the rocks below, and of wondering if you still remember the lighthouse, or if you have forgotten the name of the man who once held your hand as you stood on the shore, wide-eyed and full of wonder.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. Not a word. Not a letter. Not even a glance. And yet, every morning, I find myself standing at the window, watching the dawn break over the sea, and I wonder if you still see it the way I do. If you still feel the same awe when the first light touches the water. If you still remember the sound of my voice, or if it has been lost to the wind and the years.

The storm is coming. It is not the kind of storm that merely shakes the lighthouse; it is the kind that seeks to break it. I have seen such storms before, and I know the sea will not be

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I sit by the great lamp, its flame flickering in the wind, as if it too knows the hour is late and the night grows darker still. The sea howls like a beast in torment, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that makes me tremble. I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years, and I have seen many storms, but never one like this. I fear the heavens themselves are in revolt, and I wonder if I shall see the dawn.

It is with a heavy heart that I write to you, for we have not spoken in five years, and I do not know if I shall live to see another. You were once my greatest joy, my little light in the darkness, and I have often wondered if I have been a poor father, or if the world has been too cruel to you. I have no answers, only regret.

I know you have chosen your own path, and I have chosen mine. I have remained here, in this lonely place, tending to the light, while you have gone your way, seeking something beyond this small, quiet life. I do not blame you, for I have seen how the world is full of wonders, and how a young heart may be drawn to them. But I have also seen how the world can be cruel, and how the light of a mother’s love may be snuffed out by the winds of time and distance.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The sea roars in protest, and the sky is a bruised shade of black, as if the heavens themselves are weeping. I’ve spent most of the day watching the waves, knowing that tonight, the storm will come with a fury I’ve not seen in years. I don’t know if I’ll make it through it, and if I do, I don’t know if I’ll be able to say what I need to say.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left this house, that warm, familiar place where we once laughed and dreamed of the sea. Five years since you closed the door and walked away, not looking back. I’ve tried to forget you, but the memory of your voice, your laughter, your presence—those things have never left me. I’ve kept the lighthouse, not just for the light, but for the silence it offers. The silence where your voice once echoed.

I’ve been so angry, Eleanor. Angry at you, angry at myself, angry at the world that took you from me. I tried to be the father you needed, but I failed. I tried to be the man you deserved, but I fell short. I was too stubborn to ask for help, too proud to admit I needed you. And when you left, I told

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy as the storm clouds that now gather over the cliffs. The wind howls like a beast in torment, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very rocks upon which I stand. I fear I may not see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five years of silence, of longing, of nights where I would rise to the light and stare out at the horizon, hoping against hope that the shape of your boat might appear on the horizon. But it never did. And I have not spoken to you since the day you called me a *wretched man* and turned your back upon me.

I know the words I once spoke were cruel, that I was blind to the pain I caused you. I was lost in my duty, in the light, in the belief that I was protecting you from the darkness of this world. I was wrong. I was selfish. And I have paid the price in your silence.

This storm—this terrible, relentless storm—has come upon us with a fury I have not seen in decades. The lighthouse stands strong, but I fear it may not hold. I have seen the waves, Eliza. They rise like mountains, and I know that if the light goes out, if the beacon fails, the ships will be lost, and the lives of many will be claimed by the sea.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

It is with a heart heavy as the leaden sky above me that I take up my quill to write to you. Five long years have passed since the last time we spoke, and though the days have been long and the nights have been longer, I find myself haunted by the silence between us. The wind howls through the stones of this lighthouse as though it, too, longs to speak to you.

I write to you now not only because the storm that brews on the horizon threatens to consume all that is familiar to me, but because I fear I may not live to see another dawn. The sea has been restless for days, and the waves have grown bolder, crashing against the cliffs with a fury that reminds me of the tempest within my soul. I have seen the light of the lighthouse flicker and fade, and I fear that, like the light, my time here may be coming to an end.

You were once the light of my life, Eliza. I remember the days when we would sit by the fire, and you would tell me stories of the world beyond these cliffs—of the city, the gardens, the music, the life you longed for. I used to dream of the day when you would return to us, to see the sea from the window of our home, to hear the waves as they did in the old days. But time, like the tide, has moved on

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a beast outside these walls, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn. But if I do, I hope these words find you, and that you will read them with the same care you once gave me.

I have kept this lighthouse for over thirty years, and though it has been my home, it has also been a prison. I have watched the stars for so long that I have forgotten their names. I have tended the flame, but my soul has grown cold. You were the last light I had, Eliza, and I let it fade.

I know you do not wish to hear from me. I know you have moved on, and perhaps you have forgotten the man I once was. But I cannot let this silence remain. I have spent five years haunted by the thought of you, of the life we might have had, of the choices I made that led us here. I have not been a father to you, and I have not been a husband to your mother. I have been a man who let fear and pride turn his back on the only family he had.

**[Sealed with wax, the letter bears the crest of a lighthouse, the ink dark and smudged from the damp air.]**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these stone walls. The sea roars, and the sky is a bruised mass of black and silver. I do not know if I shall see morning. But if I do, I must speak.

Five years have passed since your last letter, and I have not spoken to you in that time. I do not know if you still remember me, or if you have forgotten the man who once held you close in the night, who once sang you lullabies as the storm raged outside.
